title: Procedures, Written Instructions, and Industry Standards
narrationScript: Every magnetic particle test you will ever run is decided on paper before the first ampere flows. In this lesson we follow the chain of documents that tells you how to magnetize a part, what to apply, what to look for, and what to do with what you find. Get comfortable with that chain, because working outside it is the fastest way to turn a good inspection into a worthless one. At the top sits the governing code or customer specification. A structural welding code, a pressure vessel code, a pipeline standard, an aerospace standard practice. These documents tell your company what the examination must accomplish and what is acceptable. They rarely tell you which button to press. That translation is the job of your employer's written procedure. A Level Three takes the code requirement and turns it into a procedure that names the equipment, the magnetization methods, the current types, the particles, the lighting, the verification checks, and the acceptance criteria reference. The procedure is qualified, approved, and revision controlled. If the code is the law, the procedure is the playbook. Below the procedure sits the written instruction, sometimes called a technique sheet or technique card. This is the part specific recipe. It says this part, this machine, this shot sequence, this amperage, these particles, this bath concentration, these light levels, this demagnetization step. As a Level One you work to written instructions, and you do not improvise. As a Level Two you can set up and run from the procedure, but the same rule holds. The numbers on the sheet were proven with shims or a gaussmeter when the technique was qualified. Change the spacing, change the amperage, or skip a shot, and that proof no longer covers you. Why does the paper care so much about method selection? Because flaw detection lives and dies on field direction. A discontinuity shows best when flux crosses it at forty five to ninety degrees. So the procedure forces two roughly perpendicular magnetizations, a circular shot for longitudinal flaws and a longitudinal shot for transverse flaws, and it picks the technique, head shot, central conductor, prods, coil, or yoke, to suit the part shape, the surface condition, and the rules of the industry you are in. Now the industries. Welds first. Structural work is commonly governed by structural welding codes, and most field weld inspection runs with an alternating current yoke and visible particles over the weld and the heat affected zone, with the yoke turned roughly ninety degrees between passes. Pressure vessels and piping typically take their examination method from the boiler code's examination article and their acceptance from the construction code. Pipeline girth welds follow pipeline welding standards. Prods are allowed in some shops for heavy work, but remember the arc strike risk, and remember that many owners ban prods on finished or fracture critical surfaces. Pipe and tubular products are their own world. Mills and inspection shops work line pipe, casing, tubing, and drill string under purchaser standards. Threaded connections and end areas get the closest attention, usually with coils or central conductors and wet fluorescent particles, because a seam or a fatigue crack in a connection can cost an entire string. Aerospace runs the tightest practice of all. The common umbrella is an industry standard practice for magnetic particle testing, layered with engine and airframe manual requirements. Expect wet fluorescent on bench units, no prods on flight hardware, strict current control, shim or gaussmeter verification of the field, careful demagnetization, and heavy documentation. Other users are everywhere once you look. Railroads check axles and wheels. Crane hooks and rigging hardware get periodic checks. Automotive plants screen crankshafts and steering forgings at production rates. Each industry has its own paper, and the pattern repeats. A code sets the goal, a procedure sets the method, a written instruction sets the numbers. So here is the habit to build. Before you touch the part, read the technique sheet. Confirm the revision. Confirm the machine, the particles, and the batch numbers match it. Run the required verification. And when something on the part does not match the paper, stop and ask. The documents are not red tape. They are the test.
imagePrompt: A magnetic particle technician in safety glasses studies an open procedure binder resting on the rail of a wet horizontal bench unit in a fabrication shop, a steel shaft clamped between the contact heads behind him.

sections.heading: The Document Chain That Runs Every Test
sections.body:
No magnetization method is ever chosen at the bench. By the time a part reaches you, a chain of documents has already decided how it will be magnetized, with what current, which particles, and what happens to anything you find. At the top of the chain is the **governing code or specification** — the contract document the customer invokes: a structural welding code, a pressure-vessel code, a pipeline standard, an aerospace standard practice. It defines what the examination must accomplish and what is acceptable, but rarely how to run your machine.

Translating that requirement is the job of the **employer's written MT procedure**, prepared and approved by a Level III, qualified by demonstration, and revision-controlled. The procedure names equipment, magnetization techniques, current types, particle systems, lighting minimums, verification checks, demagnetization requirements, and the acceptance criteria reference.

Below the procedure sits the **written instruction** — often called a technique sheet or technique card — the part-specific recipe a technician works to directly: this machine, this shot sequence, this amperage, this bath, these light readings, this demag step. SNT-TC-1A-style and NAS410-style programs are explicit about roles: a Level I performs examinations to written instructions; a Level II sets up, runs, interprets, and reports under the procedure. Nobody improvises.

sections.caption: The MT document chain: code requirements flow down through the qualified procedure and the technique sheet to the inspection record.

sections.heading: Why the Paper Picks the Method
sections.body:
The reason method selection lives in documents and not in habit is physics. A discontinuity produces a strong indication only when flux crosses it at roughly **45–90 degrees**; a flaw lying parallel to the flux barely leaks at all. So every complete technique includes **two roughly perpendicular magnetizations** — circular (head shot, central conductor, prods, induced current) for longitudinal flaws, and longitudinal (coil, cable wrap, yoke) for transverse flaws. The technique sheet picks *which* circular and *which* longitudinal technique based on part geometry, surface condition, throughput, and what the industry's rules allow — prods banned on finished aerospace surfaces because of arc-strike risk, for example.

The numbers on the sheet — amperages, ampere-turns, shot counts and times — are **typical procedure values proven during qualification**, usually with QQI artificial-flaw shims or a Hall-effect gaussmeter (procedures commonly target a tangential surface field on the order of 30–60 gauss). Treat them as binding: stretch prod spacing from six inches to eight without raising current and the field falls below what was qualified. When the sheet and the part disagree — different geometry, a coating, a prior repair — you stop and refer up. A deviation is a procedure change, and procedure changes belong to the Level III.

sections.caption: Anatomy of a part-specific technique sheet — every block exists because a code or procedure requires it.

sections.title: Typical values; the paper governs
sections.body: Every amperage, spacing, concentration, and light level quoted in this course is a **typical procedure value**. Your written procedure and technique sheet govern — when they differ from a rule of thumb, the paper wins, because the paper was qualified on your parts with your equipment and proven with shims or a gaussmeter.

sections.heading: Welds: Structural, Pressure, Pipeline
sections.body:
**Structural welds.** Buildings, bridges, and heavy weldments are commonly governed by structural welding codes such as AWS D1.1. The workhorse field technique is an **AC yoke with visible particles** over the weld and heat-affected zone, two passes with the yoke rotated roughly 90 degrees between them. Verify yoke lifting power on schedule — about **10 lb for AC** and about **40 lb for DC** at the pole spacing used.

**Pressure work.** Boiler, pressure-vessel, and piping construction typically invokes ASME Section V, Article 7 for *how* to examine, with acceptance criteria from the construction code (Section VIII, Section I, B31 piping).

**Pipelines.** Girth welds on cross-country lines follow pipeline welding standards such as API 1104, usually with portable yokes and visible or fluorescent media.

**Prods** still appear on heavy weldments where permitted — typical procedure values run on the order of **90–125 A per inch of prod spacing** at spacings of 6–8 in. — but every prod shot carries arc-strike risk at the contact points, and many owners prohibit prods on finished or fracture-critical surfaces outright.

sections.heading: Pipe and Tubular Goods
sections.body:
Tubular products run from line pipe to casing, tubing, and drill string, and the mill or inspection shop works to purchaser specifications — API 5L-style documents for line pipe, API 5CT-style for casing and tubing — plus operator drill-string inspection programs. The MT emphasis sits in two places: **seams** in the pipe body, which are longitudinal and therefore circular-magnetization targets, and **end areas and threaded connections**, where service fatigue concentrates.

Couplings and connection ID surfaces are natural **central-conductor** work: the conductor puts the strongest circular field on the inside diameter without any contact current, and an offset conductor covers roughly **four times its own diameter** of circumference per shot, rotated with overlap until the full circle is examined. Wet fluorescent particles are the norm in tubular shops because they resolve the fine, tight flaws that matter in a string under load. Pipe work is high-volume and intensely procedural — shot counts, overlaps, and rotation steps are written down precisely because crews repeat them hundreds of times a shift.

sections.heading: Aerospace and Everyone Else
sections.body:
Aerospace runs the tightest MT practice. The umbrella document most primes and OEMs invoke is a standard practice of the **ASTM E1444 type**, layered with engine- and airframe-manual requirements and audited under industry programs. Expect: **wet fluorescent on horizontal bench units**, continuous method, **no prods on flight hardware** (an arc strike on a finished, fatigue-critical surface is itself a defect), QQI shim or gaussmeter field verification, controlled demagnetization with verification (commonly to about 3 gauss or less, or per procedure), and documentation down to bath concentration and meter serial numbers.

**Other users** are everywhere: railroads examine axles, wheels, and couplers; crane hooks and lifting hardware get periodic MT under rigging-inspection rules; automotive plants screen crankshafts, spindles, and steering forgings at production rates; shipyards and military depots work to marine and defense documents. Where no industry code is invoked, general practices such as the **ASTM E709 guide** and the **ISO 9934 series** shape the procedure. Different paper, same pattern: the code sets the goal, the procedure sets the method, the written instruction sets the numbers.

sections.caption: Where common industries take their MT rules — representative documents, cited generically.
rows: Structural welding codes such as AWS D1.1
AC yoke, visible particles, weld + HAZ, two field directions
ASME Section V Art. 7 (method) + construction code (acceptance)
Yoke or prods where permitted; continuous method; documented technique
Portable yoke, visible or fluorescent media, field conditions
Purchaser specs such as API 5CT; operator inspection programs
Coil / central conductor on ends and connections; wet fluorescent
Wet fluorescent bench units; prods prohibited; strict demag and records
Baseline for writing the shop procedure

checkQuestions.question: Your shop wins a contract requiring MT per a pressure-vessel code. Who translates that requirement into the specific equipment settings and techniques used on the floor?
options: The code committee that wrote the requirement
The employer's Level III, through a qualified, revision-controlled written procedure
Each Level I technician, based on experience
The customer's purchasing agent
checkQuestions.explanation: Codes state what must be achieved; the employer's Level III converts that into a qualified written procedure and part-specific technique sheets that technicians execute.

checkQuestions.question: A technique sheet specifies prods at 100 amperes per inch of prod spacing. With the prods set 6 inches apart, what current do you use?
checkQuestions.explanation: Prod current scales with spacing: 100 A/in. × 6 in. = 600 A. Typical procedure values run on the order of 90–125 A per inch of spacing, and the qualified sheet value governs.

checkQuestions.question: Girth welds on a cross-country pipeline are most commonly examined to which kind of document?
options: A structural building welding code
A pipeline welding standard such as API 1104
An aerospace standard practice such as ASTM E1444
No standard applies to field welds
checkQuestions.explanation: Pipeline girth welds fall under pipeline welding standards of the API 1104 type, which set both the examination conditions and the acceptance criteria.

checkQuestions.question: Which combination best describes typical aerospace MT practice on finished flight hardware?
options: Dry powder applied with prods at maximum available current
Wet fluorescent particles on bench units, with prods prohibited
Visible wet particles outdoors in ambient daylight
Residual method with permanent magnets only
checkQuestions.explanation: Aerospace practices of the ASTM E1444 type center on wet fluorescent bench examination with verified fields and strict demagnetization; prods are banned on flight hardware because an arc strike is itself a defect.

checkQuestions.question: The part in front of you has a coating and a geometry that do not match the technique sheet. The correct action is to:
options: Adjust the amperage by feel and proceed
Use the closest similar technique sheet from another part
Stop and refer to supervision or the Level III — a deviation is a procedure change
Skip the second magnetizing shot to save time
checkQuestions.explanation: Technique values were qualified for a specific part and condition. Anything outside the sheet requires a controlled change by the Level III, not improvisation at the bench.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §10.1 (procedure and written instructions)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §10.2 (industry standards: welds, pipe, aerospace, other users)
Practices such as ASTM E709 and ASTM E1444 (generic reference)
ASME BPVC Section V Article 7; AWS D1.1; API 1104 (generic reference)
